overview.whyItMatters: GenAI can get expensive fast. Token costs add up, and inefficient architectures waste money. Understanding cost optimization is essential for sustainable GenAI applications. The best solutions balance cost with quality and latency.

concepts.title: Token Efficiency
concepts.content:
Tokens are the currency of GenAI. Every input and output token costs money. Optimizing token usage is the most direct way to reduce costs.

**Token Estimation and Tracking:**
Understand your token usage patterns. CloudWatch metrics track input/output tokens per request. Build dashboards showing token consumption by application, user, or feature. Know where tokens go before optimizing.

**Context Window Optimization:**
Context windows have limits (8K, 32K, 128K+ tokens depending on model). Don't waste context on irrelevant information. Select the most relevant content for each request. Summarize when full context isn't needed.

**Prompt Compression:**
Shorter prompts cost less. Remove redundant instructions. Use abbreviations when the model understands them. But don't sacrifice clarity—confused models produce poor outputs that need regeneration.

**Response Size Controls:**
Set max_tokens to limit output length. Don't generate 1000 tokens when 100 suffice. Be specific: 'Respond in 2-3 sentences' is cheaper than open-ended generation.

**Context Pruning:**
For multi-turn conversations, prune old context. Summarize earlier turns instead of including full history. Keep recent turns detailed, older turns summarized. Balance context quality with token cost.
keyPoints: Track token usage with CloudWatch metrics
Optimize context: only include relevant information
Set max_tokens to limit response length
concepts.examTip: 'Reduce token costs' -> context optimization, prompt compression, response limits.

concepts.title: Model Selection and Tiering
concepts.content:
Different models have different cost/capability tradeoffs. Smart model selection matches queries to appropriate models.

**Model Cascading:**
Route simple queries to cheap models, complex queries to expensive models. Most queries are simple. A classifier (fast, cheap) determines query complexity. Route accordingly.

Example tier:
- Tier 1: Claude Haiku ($0.25/$1.25 per million tokens) - FAQs, simple generation
- Tier 2: Claude Sonnet ($3/$15) - Moderate complexity, analysis
- Tier 3: Claude Opus ($15/$75) - Complex reasoning, specialized tasks

**Price-to-Performance:**
Cheaper isn't always better. A model that costs 10x more but produces correct answers on the first try may be cheaper than retry loops with a cheap model. Measure total cost including retries and error handling.

**Task-Specific Models:**
Some tasks don't need general-purpose models. Embedding models are cheap. Classification can use smaller models. Use specialized models where they suffice.

**Query Complexity Analysis:**
Build classifiers that estimate query complexity:
- Length and structure of query
- Presence of reasoning keywords
- Domain specificity
- Historical performance on similar queries
keyPoints: Cascade: simple queries to cheap models, complex to expensive
Consider total cost including retries, not just per-token cost
Use specialized models where general-purpose isn't needed
Build classifiers for query complexity routing

concepts.title: Caching for Cost Reduction
concepts.content:
Why pay to generate the same response twice? Caching stores results for reuse.

**Semantic Caching:**
Cache based on meaning, not exact match. 'What's the capital of France?' and 'Tell me France's capital city' should hit the same cache. Embedding similarity determines cache hits.

**Result Fingerprinting:**
Generate deterministic keys from query content. Exact matches hit cache directly. Faster than semantic similarity but misses paraphrases.

**Edge Caching:**
CloudFront caches API responses at the edge. Repeated queries from different users hit edge cache. Useful for common queries like FAQs.

**Prompt Caching:**
Bedrock prompt caching stores prompt prefixes. When multiple requests share a common system prompt, caching avoids reprocessing. Significant savings for applications with large system prompts.

**Cache Invalidation:**
Cached responses become stale. Knowledge bases update, models improve, context changes. Set TTLs appropriate to your data freshness requirements. Build cache invalidation into your data update pipelines.
keyPoints: Semantic caching: Match similar queries to cached responses
Prompt caching: Reuse processed prompt prefixes
Edge caching: CloudFront for geographically distributed users
Set appropriate TTLs; invalidate when data changes

concepts.title: Resource Utilization and Capacity
concepts.content:
Right-size your GenAI infrastructure. Over-provisioning wastes money; under-provisioning causes failures.

**Batching:**
Process multiple requests together for efficiency. Batch inference on SageMaker processes files of inputs. Even Bedrock benefits from batching—multiple requests in parallel saturate allocated capacity.

**Provisioned Throughput:**
Bedrock provisioned throughput pre-purchases capacity. Lower per-token cost but requires commitment. Calculate break-even point vs on-demand. Typically worthwhile at 40-60%+ utilization.

**Auto-Scaling:**
Scale capacity based on demand. SageMaker endpoint auto-scaling adjusts instance count. Configure based on GenAI patterns: scale on queue depth or token throughput, not just CPU.

**Capacity Planning:**
Forecast token usage. Peak times, growth projections, seasonal patterns. Provision appropriately. Reserved capacity saves money for predictable baselines; on-demand handles spikes.
keyPoints: Provisioned throughput: Lower cost at 40%+ utilization
Auto-scale on GenAI metrics (queue depth, tokens)
Forecast and plan capacity; mix reserved and on-demand

skillExplanations.explanation:
Token efficiency starts with measurement. CloudWatch metrics track input and output tokens. Build dashboards by application, feature, user segment. Understand where tokens are consumed before optimizing.

Context optimization is often the biggest win. Don't stuff context windows with irrelevant information. Select relevant content for each query. Use summarization for large documents.

Prompt compression reduces input tokens. Remove redundancy, use concise phrasing. But maintain clarity—ambiguous prompts cause poor outputs and regeneration cycles.

awsServices.whenToUse: Token usage tracking. Context window management. Response length controls.
Metrics dashboards for token consumption monitoring.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Model selection balances cost and capability. Cheaper models work for simple tasks. Complex tasks need capable models. Model cascading routes based on query complexity.

Price-to-performance analysis considers total cost. A model that gets it right the first time may be cheaper than multiple retries with a cheap model. Measure error rates and retry costs.

Task-specific models are often more efficient. Embedding models, classifiers, summarizers—use specialized tools where they suffice instead of general-purpose FMs.

awsServices.whenToUse: Multiple model options at different price points. Model selection based on requirements.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Resource utilization optimization matches capacity to demand. Batching improves throughput efficiency. SageMaker batch transform for bulk processing. Bedrock parallel requests for interactive workloads.

Provisioned throughput commits to capacity for lower per-token costs. Calculate utilization to determine if commitment makes sense. Typically saves money above 40-60% utilization.

Auto-scaling matches capacity to demand automatically. Configure scaling policies based on GenAI-relevant metrics: queue depth, token processing rate, latency. Generic CPU scaling often doesn't capture GenAI patterns.

awsServices.whenToUse: Batch transform for bulk processing. Endpoint auto-scaling.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Caching prevents redundant computation. Semantic caching uses embedding similarity to find matches even for paraphrased queries. ElastiCache or custom solutions store cached responses.

Prompt caching in Bedrock stores processed prompt prefixes. When multiple requests share common system prompts, significant savings. Particularly valuable for applications with large, complex system prompts.

Edge caching with CloudFront serves repeated responses from edge locations. Common queries hit cache without reaching origin. Set cache headers appropriately; invalidate when underlying data changes.

awsServices.whenToUse: Semantic caching with Redis. Store and retrieve cached responses.
Edge caching for geographically distributed users.
Prompt caching for system prompt prefix reuse.

values.Prompt Caching (Bedrock): Applications with large, shared system prompts
values.Edge Caching (CloudFront): Repeated identical requests from multiple locations

examStrategies.approach: Model cascading (cheap for simple, expensive for complex), caching (semantic, prompt, edge), token optimization (context pruning, response limits).
On-demand Bedrock for variable traffic. Provisioned throughput only if utilization justifies commitment. Auto-scaling for SageMaker endpoints.
Semantic caching for similar queries. Prompt caching for shared prefixes. Edge caching for repeated identical requests.

keyTakeaways: Track token usage with CloudWatch before optimizing
Model cascading: route simple to cheap models, complex to capable
Semantic caching avoids redundant computation for similar queries
Provisioned throughput saves money at 40%+ utilization
Prompt caching reuses processed system prompt prefixes
commonMistakes: Not measuring token usage before optimization efforts
Using the most capable model for all queries (expensive)
No caching strategy for repeated or similar queries
Provisioned throughput with low utilization (wasted commitment)
Auto-scaling on CPU instead of GenAI-specific metrics
